projects.name: BrgyLink
projects.shortDescription: A simple information and service portal designed for barangays, built using Bootstrap 5 and ASP.NET Core Razor Pages with C#. The project provides a user-friendly interface for barangay management and services.
projects.description:
The Barangay Information & Service Portal is a responsive web-based system designed to simplify barangay operations and improve access to public services. It is built with ASP.NET Core Razor Pages, C#, Bootstrap 5, and a secure role-based access structure.

Key Features:

• Resident Management — Maintain resident profiles, personal information, household details, and residency status.

• Document Services — Process applications for barangay clearances, certificates of residency, and other official documents.

• Blotter Management — Record, monitor, and manage reported incidents for improved public safety and case tracking.

• Payment System — Accept and monitor service payments through GCash and other supported payment methods.

• Reports — Generate reports for resident records, incidents, document requests, and financial transactions.

• AI FAQ Chatbot — Provide residents with automated answers to frequently asked questions at any time.

• User Access Control — Secure sensitive information using roles and permissions for administrators, barangay officials, staff, and residents.

• Announcements — Publish important news, schedules, emergency notices, and community updates.

• Searchable Records — Locate residents, documents, incidents, and transactions using multiple search criteria.

• Responsive Design — Provide a user-friendly experience across desktop computers, tablets, and mobile devices.
projects.preview: A simple information and service portal designed for barangays, built using Bootstrap 5 and ASP.NET Core Razor Pages with C#. The project provides a user-friendly interface for barangay management and services.
projects.challenge: One man developer, Lack of time due to OJT.
projects.solution: Managed my time and split tasks that is assigned day by day

projects.name: Kloudhotels
projects.shortDescription: KloudHotels is a cloud-based hotel management system that simplifies reservations, front-office operations, housekeeping, cashiering, inventory, reporting, and third-party integrations.
projects.description:
KloudHotels is a cloud-based Property Management System designed to help hotels manage their daily operations efficiently. It provides tools for handling reservations, guest check-ins and check-outs, room availability, housekeeping, cashiering, billing, inventory, reports, and other hotel activities.

The system centralizes important hotel information, allowing employees to access real-time data and coordinate tasks across different departments. It also supports integrations with online booking channels, keycard systems, payment services, point-of-sale systems, and accounting platforms.

With its user-friendly interface and automated processes, KloudHotels helps reduce manual work, minimize errors, improve staff productivity, and provide better service to guests. It is suitable for different hospitality businesses, including hotels, resorts, inns, and boutique properties.
projects.preview: A cloud-based hotel management system for handling reservations, front-office operations, housekeeping, cashiering, inventory, integrations, and reports.
RestoPlus is a restaurant point-of-sale and management system that simplifies order taking, billing, kitchen coordination, payments, inventory monitoring, and sales reporting.
projects.name: RestoPlus
projects.description:
RestoPlus is a comprehensive restaurant Point-of-Sale and management system designed to improve the speed, accuracy, and efficiency of food and beverage operations. It supports dine-in, takeout, and delivery transactions while providing tools for table management, order processing, discounts, payments, kitchen printing, cashier shifts, and sales reporting.

The system also includes a mobile order-taking application that allows restaurant staff to send customer orders directly from the dining area. Its inventory and item-availability features help monitor stocks and prevent unavailable products from being ordered.

RestoPlus can integrate with accounting and inventory platforms such as Odoo to synchronize contacts, transactions, invoices, payments, and stock information. It helps restaurants reduce manual processes, improve kitchen coordination, provide faster customer service, and maintain accurate operational records.
projects.shortDescription: A restaurant POS system for managing orders, tables, payments, kitchen printing, inventory, mobile order taking, integrations, and reports.
An Android mobile ordering application for managing tables, selecting menu items, checking availability, adding special requests, and sending orders directly to the POS and kitchen.
projects.preview: Resto Order Taker is a mobile application that allows restaurant staff to take customer orders directly at the table and send them to the RestoPlus POS system for faster and more accurate processing.
projects.description:
Resto Order Taker is an Android mobile application designed to improve the restaurant ordering process. It allows waiters and service staff to manage tables, enter the number of guests, browse menu categories, select food and beverage items, apply modifiers, add combo meals, include special instructions, and review orders before submission.

Orders entered through the application are sent directly to the RestoPlus POS system for processing and kitchen printing. This reduces manual order encoding, minimizes communication errors, improves coordination between service staff and the kitchen, and shortens customer waiting time.

The application also supports item-availability checking, order updates, device configuration, user authentication, local data storage, and transaction synchronization. Its offline capabilities allow staff to continue using essential functions during temporary network interruptions and synchronize the data once the connection becomes available.

Resto Order Taker helps restaurants provide faster service, maintain accurate customer orders, and create a more efficient connection between dining-area staff, cashiers, and kitchen personnel.
projects.name: Resto OrderTaker
projects.challenge: Lack of experience in android development

blogs.title: A Day in the Life of a Software Developer
blogs.excerpt:
My day begins at 4:00 AM with a few hours of online gaming. At 8:00 AM, I take a shower and prepare for work before arriving at the office at around 9:00 AM. During my workday, I check my assigned tasks, develop features, investigate problems, fix bugs, test my solutions, and communicate with my teammates.

I leave the office at 6:00 PM and usually go jogging to stay active and reduce stress. After jogging, I return home, cook dinner, and sometimes watch a movie. Depending on how tired I feel, I go to sleep at either 9:00 PM or 11:00 PM.

My routine gives me a healthy balance between software development, entertainment, exercise, household responsibilities, relaxation, and rest.
paragraphs: A Day in the Life of a Software Developer

Being a software developer is not only about writing code. It also involves solving problems, learning new things, fixing unexpected issues, and finding a healthy balance between work and personal life. Every workday can bring a different challenge, but I usually follow a simple daily routine that helps me stay productive and relaxed.
Starting My Day Early
My day begins earlier than most people’s. I usually wake up at around 4:00 AM. The surroundings are still quiet at this time, which gives me a few peaceful hours before I need to prepare for work.

Instead of immediately checking work-related messages or opening my programming tools, I spend this time relaxing. I usually play online games with friends or other players. Gaming allows me to enjoy myself, communicate with different people, and temporarily forget about work responsibilities.

Some people may think waking up at 4:00 AM just to play games is unusual, but it is one of the activities that helps me start the day in a good mood. It gives me personal time before the busy part of my day begins.
Preparing for Work
At around 8:00 AM, I stop playing and start getting ready for the office. I take a shower, get dressed, prepare the things I need, and make sure that I am ready for the day ahead.
This part of my morning routine helps me shift from relaxation mode into work mode. After preparing everything, I travel to the office and usually arrive at around 9:00 AM.

When I reach the office, I settle into my workspace, turn on my computer, and check for tasks that need to be completed. I may review messages, bug reports, requests, or updates from other members of the team. This gives me an idea of what I need to prioritize for the day.
Beginning My Work as a Developer

Once I understand my assigned tasks, I begin working on them. My responsibilities can change depending on the needs of the project. On some days, I may develop a new feature for a system. On other days, I may need to investigate and fix an existing problem.

Fixing software issues is not always simple. Sometimes, the reported problem is easy to reproduce and solve. However, there are also situations where an error only happens under certain conditions. In these cases, I need to carefully examine the code, review logs, check the data, and test different possible solutions.

A large part of software development involves problem-solving. Writing the code is only one part of the process. I also need to understand what caused the issue, determine the best solution, and make sure that my changes do not negatively affect other parts of the system.
Testing and Improving My Work
After making changes to the code, I test the system to confirm that everything works correctly. Testing is important because a small change can sometimes create another problem in a different module.

If the issue is still present, I return to the code and continue investigating. This process can happen several times before I find the correct solution. It requires patience, attention to detail, and logical thinking.
When I successfully fix a difficult problem, I feel a strong sense of accomplishment. It is satisfying to see a feature work correctly after spending hours analyzing and improving it.

I may also communicate with my teammates throughout the day. We discuss requirements, report our progress, ask questions, and help one another when someone encounters a difficult issue. Software development is often considered an individual activity, but teamwork and clear communication are also important parts of the job.
Finishing the Workday

My workday usually ends at around 6:00 PM. Before leaving the office, I review what I have completed and make sure that my work is properly saved. If a task is unfinished, I take note of my progress so I can continue working on it the next day.

After spending several hours sitting and looking at a computer screen, I am usually ready to step away from technology for a while. Leaving the office gives me an opportunity to relax mentally and focus on something different.
Exercising After Work

After work, I usually go jogging. This is an important part of my routine because software development requires me to sit for long periods. Jogging helps me remain physically active and gives my body the exercise it needs.
Running also helps clear my mind. While jogging, I can temporarily forget about bugs, deadlines, and unfinished tasks. I can focus on my surroundings, my breathing, and the movement of my body.
Although I sometimes feel tired after work, I usually feel better after exercising. Jogging reduces my stress and helps me transition from my professional responsibilities to my personal time.
Cooking Dinner at Home
After jogging, I return home and prepare dinner. I usually cook my own food instead of depending on takeout or ready-made meals.

Cooking is another activity that helps me relax. It allows me to focus on something practical and creative that is unrelated to software development. The meal I prepare depends on the available ingredients and what I feel like eating that evening.
After exercising, having a freshly cooked meal feels especially rewarding. It gives me the energy I need and provides another moment to slow down after a long day.
Relaxing Before Bedtime

Once I have finished eating and cleaning up, I usually spend the rest of the evening relaxing. Depending on my mood, I may watch a movie. Sometimes, I choose an action or comedy movie, while on other nights, I may prefer something more relaxing.
Watching movies helps me rest and enjoy the final hours of the day. However, I do not always stay awake until late at night. My bedtime depends on how tired I feel.

On some days, I sleep at around 11:00 PM. If the day has been particularly tiring, I may go to bed as early as 9:00 PM. Getting enough rest is important, especially because I wake up very early the following morning.
Maintaining a Balanced Lifestyle
My daily routine may appear simple, but it provides a balance between my career, hobbies, physical health, and personal responsibilities.

Playing online games gives me time to have fun. Working at the office allows me to improve my skills and solve meaningful problems. Jogging keeps me active, while cooking and watching movies help me relax at home.

Being a software developer can be challenging, especially when I encounter complicated errors or urgent tasks. However, it can also be rewarding. Every problem I solve teaches me something new and helps me become a better developer.

At the end of the day, my life is not entirely about programming. It is also about enjoying my hobbies, taking care of my health, preparing my own meals, and giving myself enough time to rest. This routine helps me prepare for another day of challenges and opportunities.

blogs.title: Debugging My Hardest Issue: The “No Rooms Available” Bug
blogs.excerpt:
The system displayed “No Rooms Available” even though vacant rooms appeared on the room calendar. After tracing the request from AngularJS to the C# API and SQL Server, I discovered that the availability query treated the checkout date as an occupied night.

Changing the overlap comparison from inclusive to exclusive and normalizing the date values fixed the issue. Rooms checking out on a particular date could then be assigned to new guests arriving on that same date.
paragraphs: One of the most difficult bugs I encountered as a software developer appeared to be a simple availability problem. A user tried to create a hotel reservation, selected the arrival and departure dates, and searched for an available room.
Instead of displaying the available room types, the system returned the message:
No Rooms Available

At first, the message seemed correct. It normally appears when all rooms are occupied, reserved, blocked, or out of service. However, the hotel staff confirmed that several rooms were vacant for the selected dates.
The room calendar also showed available rooms, but the reservation form showed none. This inconsistency made the issue difficult to understand.
Understanding the Problem
The first step was to reproduce the problem. I used the same hotel, room type, arrival date, departure date, booking source, and number of guests provided by the user.
The problem occurred exactly as reported. The room calendar displayed vacant rooms, but searching through the reservation form returned no results.

I tested other dates and noticed that the issue did not happen all the time. Some date ranges returned the correct rooms, while others displayed “No Rooms Available.” This meant that the room configuration itself was probably not the main problem.
The issue appeared only when a new guest’s arrival date was the same as another guest’s departure date.
For example:

Existing reservation: July 15 to July 17
New reservation: July 17 to July 19

From a hotel operations perspective, this should be allowed. The existing guest checks out on July 17, and the new guest can check in later that day. However, the system treated the two reservations as overlapping.
Checking the Front End
I started by checking the AngularJS code responsible for sending the availability request.
I verified the following values:

Hotel ID
Room type
Arrival date
Departure date
Number of adults and children
Reservation source
Selected rate
Booking status
The request contained the correct information. The arrival and departure dates were also displayed correctly in the user interface.
I added logs before sending the request and confirmed that AngularJS was passing the expected values to the API. This eliminated the front end as the primary cause.
Inspecting the API
Next, I checked the C# API that received the availability request. I added temporary logging to record the parameters before they were passed to the database.
The API received the correct hotel ID and date range. The date conversion also appeared correct, although some values still contained a time component.
For example:
There was no API exception, validation error, or failed database connection. The API successfully executed the availability query, but the database returned zero available rooms.
At this point, the investigation moved to SQL Server.
Investigating the Availability Query
The room availability process used several sources of information:

Room master records
Existing reservations
Room schedules
Out-of-order and out-of-service records
Room transfers
Maintenance blocks
Checked-in guests
Cancelled and failed reservations
Arrival and departure dates
The query needed to determine whether each room had an existing reservation that overlapped with the requested date range.
The original condition used logic similar to this:

WHERE ExistingFromDate <= @RequestedToDate
  AND ExistingToDate >= @RequestedFromDate
At first glance, the condition looked correct. It checked whether the two date ranges overlapped.
However, it treated the departure date as an occupied night.
Consider this existing reservation:

Arrival: July 15
Departure: July 17
And this new reservation:

Arrival: July 17
Departure: July 19
The condition evaluated the dates like this:

ExistingToDate >= RequestedFromDate
July 17 >= July 17
The result was true. Therefore, the query considered the existing reservation and the new reservation to be overlapping.
Because the hotel had several rooms checking out on the same date, the query excluded all of them. The reservation form then received an empty list and displayed “No Rooms Available.”
Why the Bug Was Complicated
The issue was difficult because the system had several different availability calculations.

The room calendar displayed occupancy by hotel night, while the reservation form used a separate SQL query. Both features were reading from the same database, but they applied different date-overlap rules.
The room calendar correctly understood that a checkout date was not an occupied night. The availability query treated both the arrival and departure dates as inclusive.
The bug was also inconsistent because it only appeared under specific conditions:

The requested arrival date matched another reservation’s departure date.
The room belonged to the requested room type.
No other completely vacant room was available.
The existing reservation was active and not cancelled.
The database value sometimes included a time component.

If another room was available, the system still returned a result, and the user would not notice the problem. The bug became visible only when the incorrectly excluded rooms were the last available rooms.
Fixing the Date-Overlap Logic
For hotel reservations, the departure date should normally be treated as an exclusive boundary. A guest occupies the room starting from the arrival date but not for the entire departure date.
The corrected overlap condition used this logic:

WHERE ExistingFromDate < @RequestedToDate
  AND ExistingToDate > @RequestedFromDate
The important change was replacing <= and >= with < and >.
Using the corrected condition:

Existing departure: July 17
Requested arrival: July 17
The comparison became:

ExistingToDate > RequestedFromDate
July 17 > July 17
The result was false, which meant the reservations did not overlap.
This allowed the room to become available for a same-day checkout and check-in.
Handling the Time Component
The date-overlap condition fixed the main problem, but I also needed to make the comparison consistent when the database contained time values.
Some reservations were saved with values such as:
Others contained actual check-in or check-out times:

Depending on the availability rules, comparing full datetime values could create unexpected results. The availability process was based on hotel dates rather than exact times, so the query needed normalized date values.
The parameters were converted consistently before performing the comparison:

DECLARE @ArrivalDate DATE = CONVERT(DATE, @RequestedFromDate);
DECLARE @DepartureDate DATE = CONVERT(DATE, @RequestedToDate);
The reservation dates used in the availability calculation were also handled using their hotel-date values. This prevented hidden time components from affecting the result.
Testing the Solution
After changing the query, I tested several scenarios.

Same-day room turnover
Existing booking: July 15 to July 17
New booking: July 17 to July 19
Expected result: Room is available
Actual result after the fix: Room is available
Actual overlapping reservation
Existing booking: July 15 to July 18
New booking: July 17 to July 19
Expected result: Room is unavailable
Actual result after the fix: Room is unavailable
Booking before an existing arrival
Existing booking: July 20 to July 22
New booking: July 18 to July 20
Expected result: Room is available
Actual result after the fix: Room is available
Booking inside an existing stay
Existing booking: July 15 to July 20
New booking: July 17 to July 19
Expected result: Room is unavailable
Actual result after the fix: Room is unavailable
Cancelled reservation
Existing booking status: Cancelled
Same requested date range
Expected result: Room is available
Actual result after the fix: Room is available
Out-of-service room
Room status: Out of service
No active reservation
Expected result: Room is unavailable
Actual result after the fix: Room is unavailable
I also compared the results from the reservation form with the room calendar. Both features finally displayed consistent availability.
Lessons I Learned

This issue taught me that a small comparison operator can have a major effect on an entire system. The difference between >= and > looked insignificant, but it determined whether the hotel could sell rooms that were actually available.

I also learned not to trust a generic error message immediately. “No Rooms Available” was only the final result shown to the user. It did not explain whether the rooms were occupied, blocked, incorrectly filtered, or removed because of a date-calculation problem.
When debugging an issue like this, it is important to follow the complete data flow:

Check the values selected by the user.
Inspect the request sent by the front end.
Verify the parameters received by the API.
Run the database query manually.
Examine every filter that removes a room.
Test the boundary conditions.
Compare the behavior of related system modules.
Confirm the fix using different booking scenarios.
The most important test cases are often found at the boundaries. In this situation, the critical boundary was the moment when one reservation ended and another began.
Conclusion

The “No Rooms Available” bug was one of the hardest issues I debugged because there was no application error or failed request. Every part of the system appeared to work correctly, but the final result was wrong.
The actual cause was an incorrect date-overlap condition that treated the checkout date as an occupied night. Hidden time values made the behavior even more difficult to trace.
After correcting the overlap logic and normalizing the dates, the reservation form displayed the proper room availability. The room calendar and reservation search also became consistent.

This experience reminded me that debugging is not only about fixing broken code. It is about understanding the business rules behind the system and making sure that the code represents those rules correctly.

profile.name: Pyanz Jheo C. Quiros
profile.intro: I build reliable business applications and clean digital experiences, turning complex requirements into software that feels simple to use.

educationHistory.description: Graduated in BSCS/Bachelor of Science in Computer Science

workExperiences.description: Developing hospitality and business systems using C#, AngularJS, SQL Server, APIs, and responsive interface design.
Accounts Handled: ACA NO Cost (Healthcare Account), ACA Helpline Account, MME Account
Delivered fully responsive, functional websites on a per-project basis handling both frontend and backend development ensuring client satisfaction and timely delivery.

Provides hands-on technical support by diagnosing and repairing computers, printers, and network peripherals. Ensures smooth daily operations through timely troubleshooting and preventive maintenance.
